title: Polenta con ragù di salsiccia
subtitle: Soft polenta with sausage and fennel seed ragù
excerpt: Creamy, buttery polenta topped with a quick sausage ragù scented with fennel seeds and chilli. The polenta takes twenty-five minutes of stirring; the ragù takes thirty. They finish at the same time if you start them together.
sourceNotes: Polenta with meat ragù is a northern Italian preparation documented throughout the Veneto, Lombardy, and Friuli, where polenta was the staple grain of the rural poor from the sixteenth century until the mid-twentieth century. Artusi discusses polenta extensively in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) and notes its importance in northern Italian rural cooking. The pairing with pork sausage and fennel seed reflects the flavour traditions of the Veneto and Lombardy, where fennel-seasoned pork sausages are a regional product.

recipe.batchNotes: The ragù freezes well for up to 3 months. Make a double batch and freeze half.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The ragù can be made 2 days ahead. The polenta must be made fresh.

content.text: Polenta is a dish that improves in direct proportion to the generosity with which you treat it. Stir in more butter than you think is right. Add more Parmesan than seems reasonable. The ratio of fat to grain determines everything. A polenta that has been treated meagrely will be gluey and flavourless; one that has been treated well will be silky, sweet, and the best thing under a rich ragù.

Use coarse polenta, not instant. Instant polenta cooks in five minutes and tastes like it. Coarse polenta needs twenty-five minutes of stirring, during which it develops a sweetness and creaminess that no shortcut can replicate. Start the ragù before the polenta and time them to finish together.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add the sausage meat in walnut-sized pieces. Leave for 2 minutes until browned on the base, then break up. Cook for 3 more minutes until cooked through. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{fennel-seeds}} of fennel seeds, and chilli flakes. Cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and let bubble for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes. Simmer for 20 minutes until the sauce is thickened and deep.

Bring 1.2 litres of water to a boil in a large saucepan with a generous pinch of salt. Pour the {{polenta}} in a thin, steady stream, whisking constantly. Once combined, switch to a wooden spoon. Cook over medium-low heat, stirring every minute, for 25 minutes until the polenta is thick, pulling away from the sides, and no longer tastes grainy. Remove from the heat. Beat in the {{unsalted-butter}} and the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan vigorously. Taste for salt.
Spoon the polenta into warm bowls and ladle the ragù generously over. Serve with extra Parmesan.

items.cause: Cooked too fast, or not enough liquid; instant polenta used
items.fix: Use coarse polenta; cook slowly over medium-low heat for the full 25 minutes; add more hot water if it stiffens

items.cause: Undersalted; too little butter or Parmesan
items.fix: Salt the water generously; the butter and Parmesan at the end are essential, not optional

items.cause: Not simmered long enough; lid left on
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for the full 20 minutes; the sauce should coat the back of a spoon

items.symptom: Polenta set and solidified before serving
items.cause: Served too slowly; polenta sets quickly once off the heat
items.fix: Have the ragù ready before finishing the polenta; serve immediately; add a splash of hot water if the polenta thickens while waiting

content.text: Polenta was the staple grain of northern Italy from the sixteenth century (after maize arrived from the Americas) until the economic transformation of the mid-twentieth century brought rice and pasta within easy reach of rural populations. During those centuries it was eaten twice a day in the Veneto, Lombardy, and Friuli, usually plain, sometimes with cheese, sometimes with whatever meat the household could afford. It is now eaten by choice rather than necessity, which has transformed it from a poverty food into something worth celebrating. The combination with pork sausage and fennel seed reflects the flavours of the same northern regions where polenta dominated: this is not the polenta of southern Italy, which uses a finer grind and a wetter consistency, but the coarse, substantial polenta of the north, served under a ragù that does not apologise for its richness.
